description: Piracy, corsair and privateering danger zones per decade, 1650-1730. intensity: 1 low, 2 serious, 3 most dangerous. Polygons are coarse sea areas, [lon,lat] WGS84. Chronology after Marcus Rediker (Villains of All Nations), David Cordingly (Under the Black Flag), and Rodger.

zones.name: Barbary corsairs — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: Algiers and Tunis cruisers take Christian shipping and slaves the length of the middle sea.

zones.name: Corsair range — Atlantic approaches
zones.note: Sailing rovers still pass the Strait toward Madeira and Iberia; the great raids to Ireland and Iceland (1620s-40s) are tapering.

zones.name: Salé rovers — Moroccan coast
zones.note: The corsair republic of Salé works the Cádiz-Canaries traffic.

zones.name: Buccaneers — Tortuga & Hispaniola coasts
zones.note: Cattle-hunters of Hispaniola turn sea-rover from Tortuga (Cordingly).

zones.name: Anglo-Dutch war — Channel & North Sea
zones.note: 1652-54: fleets and privateers of both nations make the narrow seas deadly to trade.

zones.name: Spanish Main — coastal raiding
zones.note: Small English and French raids on the Tierra Firme coast presage worse.

zones.name: Port Royal buccaneers — south Cuba cruising grounds
zones.note: Myngs sacks Santiago de Cuba 1662 and Campeche 1663 with Jamaica commissions.

zones.name: Buccaneer grounds — Windward Passage
zones.note: The chokepoint between Cuba and Hispaniola: every Spanish coaster runs the gauntlet.

zones.name: Spanish Main — Morgan's raids
zones.note: Portobelo stormed 1668, Maracaibo burned 1669.

zones.name: Gulf of Campeche — logwood coast
zones.note: Logwood cutters and raiders, half trade and half plunder.

zones.name: Barbary corsairs — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: Algiers at war with England 1668-71.

zones.name: Corsair range — Atlantic approaches
zones.note: Salé and Algiers rovers off Iberia and the islands.

zones.name: War privateers — Channel & North Sea
zones.note: Second Anglo-Dutch War 1665-67; Dunkirk capers and English privateers.

zones.name: Spanish Main — the Panama raid and after
zones.note: Morgan crosses the isthmus and burns Panama, January 1671; the Treaty of Madrid (1670) slowly turns Jamaica against its own buccaneers.

zones.name: Buccaneer grounds — Windward Passage
zones.note: Unpardoned crews shift to French commissions from Tortuga and Petit-Goâve.

zones.name: Logwood coasts — Campeche & Honduras
zones.note: Dampier's logwood men: cutters when sober, raiders when not.

zones.name: South Cuba cays
zones.note: Turtling grounds and pirate careening cays off Cuba's south shore.

zones.name: Barbary corsairs — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: Narbrough's cruisers force terms on Algiers and Tripoli.

zones.name: War privateers — Channel
zones.note: Third Anglo-Dutch War 1672-74; Zeeland privateers answer in kind.

zones.name: Corsair range — Atlantic approaches
zones.note: Declining as treaties multiply.

zones.name: South Sea buccaneers — Pacific coast of Peru & Panama
zones.note: Sharp, Davis and Dampier cross the isthmus 1680; the Mar del Sur loses its innocence (Dampier, New Voyage).

zones.name: Gulf of Mexico — Veracruz and Campeche sacked
zones.note: De Graaf and Grammont sack Veracruz 1683, Campeche 1685 — the buccaneers' last great Caribbean strokes.

zones.name: Windward Passage
zones.note: Petit-Goâve flibustiers still cruise under French paper.

zones.name: Honduras coast
zones.note: Raiders and logwood men along the Mosquito shore.

zones.name: Red Sea mouth — first Roundsmen
zones.note: A handful of Caribbean crews discover the pilgrim fleets; the Pirate Round begins.

zones.name: Barbary corsairs — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: French bombardments (1682-83) humble Algiers but cruising continues.

zones.name: Corsair range — Atlantic approaches
zones.note: Residual rover traffic off Iberia.

zones.name: Pirate Round — Madagascar & St Mary's Island
zones.note: St Mary's the pirates' bazaar; Adam Baldridge sells them powder and news (Rediker).

zones.name: Red Sea & Bab el-Mandeb — the Mocha fleet
zones.note: Henry Every takes the Ganj-i-Sawai 1695; Thomas Tew dies trying to repeat it.

zones.name: Surat & Malabar approaches
zones.note: Mughal convoys stalked; the EIC at Bombay held hostage for English pirates' sins.

zones.name: French privateers — Western Approaches & Channel
zones.note: Jean Bart, Duguay-Trouin and the Saint-Malo armateurs bleed Allied trade (Rodger).

zones.name: War privateers — Caribbean
zones.note: Nine Years War: flibustiers sack Cartagena 1697; English counter-raids.

zones.name: North American seaboard — pirate markets
zones.note: New York and Newport quietly fit out and fence for the Red Sea men; Kidd sails 1696.

zones.name: Barbary corsairs — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: Cruising against nations without treaties.

zones.name: Privateer war — Western Approaches & Channel
zones.note: War of the Spanish Succession: Dunkirk and Saint-Malo at their height; Duguay-Trouin takes whole convoys.

zones.name: Privateers — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: War cruisers of every flag joined by the unreformed corsairs.

zones.name: War privateers — Caribbean
zones.note: Piracy proper nearly vanishes — the men sail under lawful commissions instead (Rediker).

zones.name: Madagascar — the Round declines
zones.note: Kidd hanged 1701; squadrons and pardons thin the St Mary's men.

zones.name: Newfoundland — wartime raids
zones.note: French raids from Plaisance harry the fishery until Utrecht.

zones.name: Corsair range — Atlantic approaches
zones.note: Salé takes neutral and enemy small craft alike off Iberia.

zones.name: Pirate Nassau — the Bahamas
zones.note: Demobilized privateers make New Providence a pirate republic, 1713-18, until Woodes Rogers lands.

zones.name: Florida Strait — the 1715 wrecks
zones.note: The plate fleet drowns on the Florida shore; Jennings raids the Spanish salvage camps and a generation turns pirate.

zones.name: Windward Passage & Hispaniola coasts
zones.note: The old chokepoint again: Hornigold, Blackbeard and Bellamy all cruise it.

zones.name: North American seaboard
zones.note: Blackbeard blockades Charles Town 1718; Bellamy's Whydah wrecks on Cape Cod 1717.

zones.name: Leeward Islands cruising grounds
zones.note: Inter-island traders snapped up among the sugar islands.

zones.name: Guinea coast — the trade's shadow
zones.note: Howell Davis opens the African hunting grounds, 1719; slavers are easy prizes.

zones.name: Barbary corsairs — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: Treaty-bound toward Britain and France; deadly still to Italians and Iberians.

zones.name: Guinea coast — Roberts' grounds
zones.note: Bartholomew Roberts takes over 400 ships before HMS Swallow kills him off Cape Lopez, Feb 1722 (Rediker).

zones.name: Eastern Caribbean — the last great cruises
zones.note: Roberts hangs the governor of Martinique's men from his yardarm, 1721; collapse follows the mass hangings of 1722-26.

zones.name: Indian Ocean revival — Madagascar & the Mascarenes
zones.note: Taylor and Levasseur take the Cassandra (1720) and the Nossa Senhora do Cabo (1721), richest prize of the age.

zones.name: North American seaboard — Low and Lowther
zones.note: Edward Low's cruelty becomes proverbial; Greyhound's victory hangs 26 at Newport, 1723.

zones.name: Newfoundland banks
zones.note: Roberts plunders the Trepassey fishing fleet, June 1720; pirates recruit among abused fishermen.

zones.name: Windward Passage & Cuban coast
zones.note: Last crews and Spanish guarda-costas make the passage uneasy into the late 1720s.

zones.name: Bahamas — residual
zones.note: Rogers' Nassau holds; Rackham, Bonny and Read taken 1720, Vane hanged 1721.

zones.name: Barbary corsairs — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: Steady low-grade corsairing continues.

zones.name: Caribbean — guarda-costa seizures
zones.note: Piracy is dead but Spanish coast-guards seize British traders on any pretext — Jenkins loses his ear, 1731.

zones.name: Barbary corsairs — Western Mediterranean
zones.note: The one corsair institution that outlives the pirate age.

zones.name: Madagascar — remnant settlements
zones.note: Aging ex-pirates trade slaves and cattle at St Mary's; the Round is memory.

zones.name: Guinea coast — residual
zones.note: Occasional mutiny-piracies on slavers; the navy's patrols keep order.

zones.name: Florida Strait — wreckers
zones.note: Bahamian wreckers, lawful and otherwise, work the reef where the plate fleets drowned.
